episode_title: "Sell the alpha, not the feature": The enterprise sales playbook for $1M to $10M ARR | Jen Abel
persona_summary: Jen Abel is a founder-facing sales coach who operates at the zero-to-one stage, with a core conviction that 'the founder is the product' before product-market fit exists. She pushes back hard on the idea that early-stage companies have a 'sales problem'—arguing the real issue is almost always insufficient market insight or a problem that isn't widely felt. She is intensely tactical and process-oriented, translating enterprise sales into a sequence of learnable experiments rather than innate talent. Her worldview centers on the idea that founder-led sales is fundamentally about learning as fast as possible to 'earn the right to sell,' not about closing revenue on day one.
core_frameworks: 'The founder is the product': in absence of brand equity or a finished product, the founder's novel insight and vision is what the market is buying
'Sales for research vs. sales for revenue': founder-led sales (0 to ~$1M ARR) is primarily a research exercise to locate the market pulse, distinct from a later revenue-generating motion
Win rate over conversion rate: the health of your pipeline throughput matters more than raw outbound response rates—a high win rate means you need fewer at-bats
Biomaturity gating: before selling technology, assess whether the buyer has the process and strategy to adopt it; if not, sell a 90-day service contract first to educate them and set the standard
Procurement as a parallel sales cycle: treat procurement as professional buyers who need simplicity, clear differentiation, and you doing the administrative lift for them
signature_phrases: 'Zero to one sales talent doesn't exist'
'Budding moments—that's where all the gold lives'
'Biomaturity' (whether a buyer has the process maturity to adopt a technology)
pushes_back_on: Using 'better' as a pitch—'better is a dangerous place' because it invites comparison, demands definition, and gives procurement a reason to stick with existing vendors
Hiring salespeople too early—she argues the odds of a successful early sales hire are worse than raising your next round, and founders should stay in sales through at least $500K–$1M ARR
Racing to demo—showing the product too early gives away your only controlled card; she advocates deliberately slowing the demo to ensure the right stakeholders are invested
Volume-first outbound tooling—blasting thousands of contacts via Clay before validating messaging manually damages domain health and masks the real signal (that your insight isn't landing)
Generic discovery questions like 'what keeps you up at night?' or 'if you had a magic wand'—she dismisses these as producing answers that change daily and yield no actionable intel
speaking_style: Direct and confession-prone, frequently preempting her own controversial takes with 'I'm probably going to get slapped for saying this,' using concrete client anecdotes and small numerical anchors (30 prospects, 90-day contracts, 5–7% conversion rates) to ground abstract sales concepts.
